questions.title: Converse, contrapositive, and negation
questions.prompt:
Consider the statement: If n² is even, then n is even (where n is an integer).
(i) Write the converse of this statement.
(ii) Write the contrapositive of this statement.
(iii) Write the negation of this statement.
(iv) State which of the original statement and its converse are true, and which proof strategy is most naturally suited to proving the original statement.
questions.hint: The contrapositive of 'if P then Q' is 'if not Q then not P', and is logically equivalent to the original.

markscheme.text: (i) Converse: If n is even, then n² is even.
(ii) Contrapositive: If n is odd, then n² is odd. (Logically equivalent to the original.)
(iii) Negation: There exists an integer n such that n² is even but n is odd.
(iv) Both the original and its converse are true. Proof by contrapositive is most natural for the original: suppose n is odd, so n = 2k+1; then n² = 4k²+4k+1 = 2(2k²+2k)+1, which is odd.

questions.title: Direct proof of congruence preservation
questions.prompt:
Let m, n, a, b, k be integers with k > 0.
(i) State the definition of m ≡ a (mod k).
(ii) Prove directly: if m ≡ a (mod k) and n ≡ b (mod k), then m + n ≡ a + b (mod k).
(iii) Using a similar argument, prove that m · n ≡ a · b (mod k).

markscheme.text: (i) m ≡ a (mod k) means k divides (m − a), i.e. there exists an integer q such that m − a = qk.
(ii) m − a = qk and n − b = rk for some q, r ∈ ℤ. Then (m+n) − (a+b) = (m−a)+(n−b) = qk+rk = (q+r)k, so k | (m+n−a−b), i.e. m+n ≡ a+b (mod k).
(iii) mn − ab = mn − an + an − ab = n(m−a) + a(n−b) = n·qk + a·rk = (nq+ar)k, so k | (mn−ab), i.e. mn ≡ ab (mod k).

questions.title: Proof by contradiction: irrationality of √3
questions.prompt: Prove by contradiction that √3 is irrational. Your proof should clearly state the assumption, derive the contradiction, and explain how the contradiction arises.
questions.hint: Assume √3 = p/q in lowest terms. What does squaring tell you about divisibility of p by 3?

markscheme.text: Assume for contradiction that √3 is rational, so √3 = p/q where p, q ∈ ℤ, q ≠ 0, and gcd(p,q) = 1 (fraction in lowest terms).
Then 3 = p²/q², so p² = 3q², which means 3 | p².
Since 3 is prime and 3 | p², we have 3 | p. Write p = 3s for some s ∈ ℤ.
Substituting: (3s)² = 3q², so 9s² = 3q², so q² = 3s², meaning 3 | q² and hence 3 | q.
But then 3 | p and 3 | q, contradicting gcd(p,q) = 1. Therefore √3 is irrational.

questions.title: Mathematical induction
questions.prompt: This question is about proof by induction.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Prove by induction that for all n ∈ ℕ with n ≥ 1: 1 + 2 + 3 + ··· + n = n(n+1)/2. Your proof must clearly identify the base case, inductive hypothesis, and inductive step.

markscheme.text: Base case n = 1: LHS = 1; RHS = 1·2/2 = 1. ✓
Inductive hypothesis: assume the formula holds for some k ≥ 1, i.e. 1+2+···+k = k(k+1)/2.
Inductive step: 1+2+···+k+(k+1) = k(k+1)/2 + (k+1) = (k+1)(k/2+1) = (k+1)(k+2)/2, which is the formula for n = k+1. By induction, the result holds for all n ≥ 1.

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Prove by induction that n! > 2ⁿ for all integers n ≥ 4.

markscheme.text: Base case n = 4: 4! = 24 > 16 = 2⁴. ✓
Inductive hypothesis: assume k! > 2ᵏ for some k ≥ 4.
Inductive step: (k+1)! = (k+1)·k! > (k+1)·2ᵏ ≥ 2·2ᵏ = 2ᵏ⁺¹, where the first inequality uses the inductive hypothesis and the second uses k+1 ≥ 5 > 2. By induction, n! > 2ⁿ for all n ≥ 4.

questions.title: Fermat's Little Theorem: statement, application, and proof
questions.prompt: This question concerns Fermat's Little Theorem.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: State Fermat's Little Theorem. Write down what it implies about i^p mod p for any integer i and prime p.

markscheme.text: Fermat's Little Theorem: for all integers i and primes p, i^p ≡ i (mod p).
Equivalently: if p does not divide i then i^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p). In either case, i^p mod p = i mod p.

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Use Fermat's Little Theorem to compute (i) 7¹⁰⁰ mod 13 and (ii) 3⁵⁰ mod 7. Show your working.

markscheme.text: (i) By FLT, 7¹² ≡ 1 (mod 13). Write 100 = 8×12 + 4, so 7¹⁰⁰ ≡ 7⁴ (mod 13). Now 7² = 49 ≡ 10 (mod 13) and 7⁴ ≡ 100 ≡ 9 (mod 13). Answer: 9.
(ii) By FLT, 3⁶ ≡ 1 (mod 7). Write 50 = 8×6 + 2, so 3⁵⁰ ≡ 3² = 9 ≡ 2 (mod 7). Answer: 2.

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Prove Fermat's Little Theorem — that i^p ≡ i (mod p) for all integers i ≥ 0 and all primes p — by induction on i. You may use the Binomial Theorem and the fact that for a prime p and 1 ≤ k ≤ p−1, the binomial coefficient C(p,k) is divisible by p.

markscheme.text: Base case i = 0: 0^p = 0 ≡ 0 (mod p). ✓
Inductive hypothesis: assume k^p ≡ k (mod p) for some k ≥ 0.
Inductive step: by the Binomial Theorem, (k+1)^p = Σⱼ₌₀ᵖ C(p,j) kʲ = k^p + C(p,1)k^(p-1) + ··· + C(p,p-1)k + 1.

For 1 ≤ j ≤ p−1, p | C(p,j) since p is prime (C(p,j) = p!/(j!(p-j)!) and p divides the numerator but not the denominator). All middle terms vanish mod p, giving (k+1)^p ≡ k^p + 1 ≡ k + 1 (mod p) by the inductive hypothesis. By induction, i^p ≡ i (mod p) for all i ≥ 0.

questions.title: Euclid's algorithm
questions.prompt: Use Euclid's algorithm to compute gcd(252, 105). Show every step of the algorithm as a sequence of division equations a = q·b + r. State the final answer and express gcd(252, 105) as an integer linear combination of 252 and 105.
questions.hint: The algorithm terminates when the remainder is 0; the GCD is the last non-zero remainder. Back-substitute to find the linear combination.

questions.title: Multiplicative inverse via Extended Euclid
questions.prompt: Find the multiplicative inverse of 17 in ℤ₄₁ (the integers modulo 41). Use the Extended Euclid's algorithm, showing all steps. Verify your answer by checking that 17 × (your answer) ≡ 1 (mod 41).
questions.hint: The inverse of [n]ₘ exists iff gcd(m,n)=1. Run Euclid's algorithm on (41, 17) then back-substitute to write 1 as a linear combination of 41 and 17.

markscheme.text: Euclid's algorithm: 41 = 2×17 + 7; 17 = 2×7 + 3; 7 = 2×3 + 1; 3 = 3×1 + 0. So gcd(41, 17) = 1 (inverse exists).

questions.title: Induction in number theory
questions.prompt: This question concerns induction arguments in number theory.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Prove by induction that for all n ∈ ℕ (n ≥ 0), the expression n³ + 2n is divisible by 3.

markscheme.text: Base case n = 0: 0³ + 2·0 = 0 = 3·0. Divisible by 3. ✓
Inductive hypothesis: assume k³ + 2k = 3m for some m ∈ ℤ and k ≥ 0.
Inductive step: (k+1)³ + 2(k+1) = k³ + 3k² + 3k + 1 + 2k + 2 = (k³ + 2k) + 3(k² + k + 1) = 3m + 3(k²+k+1) = 3(m+k²+k+1), which is divisible by 3. By induction the result holds for all n ≥ 0.

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: State the Principle of Strong Induction. Use it to prove that every integer n ≥ 2 has at least one prime factor.

markscheme.text: Strong induction from basis ℓ: if P(ℓ) holds, and for any n ≥ ℓ, (P(k) for all ℓ ≤ k ≤ n) implies P(n+1), then P(m) holds for all m ≥ ℓ.

Proof: Base n=2: 2 is prime so 2 is its own prime factor. Inductive step: suppose every integer in [2..n] has a prime factor. If n+1 is prime, it is its own factor. If composite, n+1 = a·b with 2 ≤ a ≤ n; by hypothesis a has a prime factor p; since p|a and a|(n+1), p|(n+1). Done.

questions.title: GCD characterisation and modular inverses
questions.prompt: This question concerns the GCD Characterisation Theorem and its applications.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: State the GCD Characterisation Theorem (Theorem 92 in the lectures). What does it say about the relationship between gcd(m, n) and integer linear combinations of m and n?

markscheme.text: For all positive integers m and n, gcd(m, n) is the least positive integer expressible as am + bn for integers a, b (the least positive linear combination of m and n).
Equivalently: {am+bn | a,b ∈ ℤ} = {k·gcd(m,n) | k ∈ ℤ} — every linear combination of m and n is a multiple of gcd(m,n), and gcd(m,n) itself is achievable.

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Use the Extended Euclid's algorithm to express gcd(35, 22) as an integer linear combination of 35 and 22. Show all steps.

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Using the result of part (b), find the multiplicative inverse of [22]₃₅ in ℤ₃₅. State why the inverse exists and verify your answer.

markscheme.text: The inverse exists because gcd(35,22) = 1 (from part b), so 22 and 35 are coprime.
From part (b): 1 = 8×22 − 5×35, so 8×22 ≡ 1 (mod 35). The inverse of [22]₃₅ is [8]₃₅. Verify: 22×8 = 176 = 5×35+1 ≡ 1 (mod 35). ✓

questions.title: Diffie-Hellman and RSA cryptography
questions.prompt: This question concerns public-key cryptography.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Describe the Diffie-Hellman key exchange scheme. Work through an example with public parameters prime p = 11, base c = 2, Alice's secret a = 3, and Bob's secret b = 6. Show all intermediate values and confirm both parties obtain the same shared secret.

markscheme.text: Alice computes α = c^a mod p = 2³ mod 11 = 8 and sends α to Bob. Bob computes β = c^b mod p = 2⁶ mod 11 = 64 mod 11 = 9 and sends β to Alice.
Alice computes k = β^a mod p = 9³ mod 11 = 729 mod 11 = 3. Bob computes k = α^b mod p = 8⁶ mod 11: 8² ≡ 9, 8³ ≡ 72 ≡ 6, 8⁶ ≡ 36 ≡ 3 (mod 11). Both obtain shared secret k = 3. ✓
Security: an eavesdropper who sees α = 8 and β = 9 must solve the discrete logarithm problem (find a with 2^a ≡ 8 mod 11), which is computationally hard for large primes p.

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: An RSA scheme uses prime p = 11 and encryption exponent e = 3. (i) Verify gcd(p−1, e) = 1. (ii) Find the decryption exponent d. (iii) Encrypt the message k = 5, obtaining ciphertext ℓ, then decrypt ℓ to recover 5.

markscheme.text: (ii) d = inverse of e = 3 in ℤ₁₀. Need 3d ≡ 1 (mod 10). Since 3×7 = 21 ≡ 1 (mod 10), d = 7.
(iii) Encrypt: ℓ = 5³ mod 11 = 125 mod 11 = 4. Decrypt: 4⁷ mod 11: 4² ≡ 5, 4⁴ ≡ 3, 4⁷ = 4⁴·4²·4 ≡ 3·5·4 = 60 ≡ 5 (mod 11). Recovered k = 5. ✓

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Prove that RSA decryption always recovers the original message: (k^e)^d ≡ k (mod p). Cite Fermat's Little Theorem explicitly. (You may assume p does not divide k.)

markscheme.text: By construction, ed ≡ 1 (mod p−1), so ed = 1 + t(p−1) for some integer t ≥ 0.
By Fermat's Little Theorem, k^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p) (since p is prime and p ∤ k). Therefore k^(ed) = k^(1+t(p−1)) = k · (k^(p−1))^t ≡ k · 1 = k (mod p).
Hence the decryption exponent d perfectly inverts the encryption: (k^e)^d ≡ k (mod p). ✓

questions.title: Set operations and De Morgan's law
questions.prompt:
Let U = {1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8} be the universal set, A = {1,2,3,5}, and B = {2,4,5,6}.
(i) Compute A ∩ B and (A ∩ B)ᶜ (the complement with respect to U).
(ii) Compute Aᶜ and Bᶜ, and then compute Aᶜ ∪ Bᶜ.
(iii) State De Morgan's law relating (A ∩ B)ᶜ and Aᶜ ∪ Bᶜ, and verify it holds here.

markscheme.text: (iii) De Morgan's law: (A ∩ B)ᶜ = Aᶜ ∪ Bᶜ. Both sides give {1,3,4,6,7,8}. ✓

questions.title: Injective, surjective, bijective functions
questions.prompt:
For each of the following functions f : ℤ → ℤ, determine whether it is (a) injective (one-to-one), (b) surjective (onto), and hence (c) bijective. Justify each answer.
(i) f(n) = n + 1
(ii) f(n) = n²
(iii) f(n) = 2n

markscheme.text: (i) f(n)=n+1: Injective (n+1=m+1 implies n=m). Surjective (for any y ∈ ℤ, f(y−1)=y). Bijective.
(ii) f(n)=n²: Not injective (f(1)=f(−1)=1 but 1≠−1). Not surjective (−1 has no preimage). Not bijective.
(iii) f(n)=2n: Injective (2n=2m implies n=m). Not surjective (1 ∈ ℤ has no preimage as 2n is always even). Not bijective.

questions.title: Counting functions, injections, and surjections
questions.prompt:
Let A = {a, b, c} and B = {0, 1}. Using the lecture cardinality formulae, compute:
(i) The number of functions f : A → B.
(ii) The number of injections A ↪ B.
(iii) The number of surjections A ↠ B. (Use Stirling numbers: S(3,2) = 3.)
(iv) The cardinality of 𝒫(A × B), the powerset of A × B.

markscheme.text: (ii) No injections exist: |A|=3 > |B|=2, so we cannot map 3 distinct elements to 2 distinct values injectively. #Inj(A,B) = 0.
(iii) #Sur(A,B) = S(|A|,|B|)·|B|! = S(3,2)·2! = 3·2 = 6. (The 3 partitions of {a,b,c} into 2 non-empty blocks are {a},{b,c}; {b},{a,c}; {c},{a,b}; each gives 2! assignments.)

questions.title: Element-chasing proof of distributivity
questions.prompt:
Prove the following set identity by element chasing (showing each side is a subset of the other):
A ∩ (B ∪ C) = (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C)
for any sets A, B, C in a universe U.
questions.hint: To show X = Y, show X ⊆ Y and Y ⊆ X. For each, take an arbitrary element of the left side and show it belongs to the right side.

markscheme.text: (⊆) Let x ∈ A ∩ (B ∪ C). Then x ∈ A and x ∈ B ∪ C, so x ∈ A and (x ∈ B or x ∈ C).
If x ∈ B then x ∈ A ∩ B ⊆ (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C). If x ∈ C then x ∈ A ∩ C ⊆ (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C). In both cases x ∈ RHS.
(⊇) Let x ∈ (A∩B) ∪ (A∩C). Either x ∈ A∩B (so x ∈ A and x ∈ B ⊆ B∪C) or x ∈ A∩C (so x ∈ A and x ∈ C ⊆ B∪C). In both cases x ∈ A and x ∈ B∪C, so x ∈ A∩(B∪C). Hence equality.

questions.title: Equivalence relations and quotient sets
questions.prompt: This question concerns equivalence relations.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define what it means for a binary relation E on a set A to be an equivalence relation. State all three required properties.

markscheme.text: E ⊆ A × A is an equivalence relation if it is: (1) Reflexive: ∀a ∈ A, a E a; (2) Symmetric: ∀a,b ∈ A, a E b → b E a; (3) Transitive: ∀a,b,c ∈ A, (a E b ∧ b E c) → a E c.

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Define the relation E on ℤ by m E n iff 3 | (m − n) (i.e. m ≡ n mod 3). Prove that E is an equivalence relation by verifying all three properties.

markscheme.text: Reflexive: m − m = 0 = 3·0, so 3 | (m−m). Thus m E m for all m ∈ ℤ. ✓
Symmetric: if m E n then 3 | (m−n), so m−n = 3k. Then n−m = −3k = 3(−k), so 3 | (n−m), giving n E m. ✓
Transitive: if m E n and n E p then m−n = 3k and n−p = 3j. Then m−p = (m−n)+(n−p) = 3(k+j), so 3 | (m−p), giving m E p. ✓

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Describe the equivalence classes of E. How many are there?

markscheme.text: There are exactly 3 equivalence classes: [0] = {…,−6,−3,0,3,6,…}, [1] = {…,−5,−2,1,4,7,…}, [2] = {…,−4,−1,2,5,8,…}. These partition ℤ.

questions.title: Countability and Cantor's theorem
questions.prompt: This question concerns infinite cardinalities.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Define what it means for a set A to be (i) enumerable and (ii) countable. Show that the set of all integers ℤ is countable by exhibiting an explicit surjection f : ℕ → ℤ.

markscheme.text: A is enumerable if there exists a surjection ℕ ↠ A. A is countable if it is either empty or enumerable.
Define f(0)=0, f(2k−1)=k, f(2k)=−k for k ≥ 1. This enumerates ℤ as 0, 1, −1, 2, −2, 3, −3, … covering every integer. So ℤ is enumerable (countable).

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Prove that ℕ × ℕ is countable by describing an explicit enumeration (surjection ℕ → ℕ × ℕ).

markscheme.text: Use Cantor's diagonal enumeration: list pairs by diagonals of constant sum m+n = 0, 1, 2, …: (0,0); then (0,1),(1,0); then (0,2),(1,1),(2,0); and so on.
Every pair (m,n) appears on the diagonal m+n = m+n and is listed at a finite position. This gives a well-defined surjection ℕ → ℕ×ℕ.
Since there is a surjection ℕ → ℕ×ℕ, the set ℕ×ℕ is enumerable and hence countable. (Also injective, so it is a bijection, giving ℕ×ℕ ≅ ℕ.)

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Prove Cantor's theorem: for any set A, there is no surjection A ↠ 𝒫(A). Deduce that 𝒫(ℕ) is uncountable.

markscheme.text: Suppose for contradiction f : A → 𝒫(A) is a surjection. Define the diagonal set D = {a ∈ A | a ∉ f(a)}. Then D ⊆ A, so D ∈ 𝒫(A).
Since f is surjective, ∃d ∈ A with f(d) = D. Ask: is d ∈ D? If d ∈ D, then by definition d ∉ f(d) = D — contradiction. If d ∉ D, then by definition d ∈ D — contradiction. Either case is impossible.
Hence no surjection A → 𝒫(A) exists. In particular, no surjection ℕ → 𝒫(ℕ) exists, so 𝒫(ℕ) is not enumerable, i.e. 𝒫(ℕ) is uncountable.

questions.title: Tracing a DFA
questions.prompt:
Consider the DFA M with states {q₀, q₁, q₂}, alphabet Σ = {a, b}, start state q₀, accepting states {q₂}, and the following transition function:

Stateab
q₀q₁q₀
q₁q₁q₂
q₂q₁q₀

(i) Trace M on the input aab, listing the state after each character. Does M accept?
(ii) Trace M on the input bab. Does M accept?
(iii) Give an informal description of the language L(M).

markscheme.text: (i) Start q₀. Read a → q₁. Read a → q₁. Read b → q₂. Final state q₂ ∈ {q₂}, so M accepts 'aab'. ✓
(ii) Start q₀. Read b → q₀. Read a → q₁. Read b → q₂. Final state q₂. M accepts 'bab'. ✓

(iii) M accepts exactly the strings over {a,b} that end with 'ab' (the suffix is exactly one or more a's followed by a single b, with the final two characters being 'ab'). Equivalently: strings whose last character is b immediately preceded by at least one a.

questions.title: Constructing a DFA for divisibility by 3
questions.prompt:
Construct a DFA over Σ = {0, 1} that accepts exactly the binary strings whose numeric value is divisible by 3 (the empty string represents 0, which is divisible by 3).
(i) Describe what each state represents and explain the transition rule.
(ii) Give the complete transition function as a table.
(iii) Verify your DFA on input 110 (= 6, divisible by 3) and on 101 (= 5, not divisible by 3).
questions.hint: If the current value is v and you read bit b, the new value is 2v + b. Consider what (2v + b) mod 3 equals in terms of (v mod 3) and b.

markscheme.text: States q₀, q₁, q₂ where qᵣ means 'the binary value read so far ≡ r (mod 3)'. Start and sole accepting state: q₀. On reading bit b in state qᵣ, go to state q_{(2r+b) mod 3}.
Transition table: q₀ -0→ q₀, q₀ -1→ q₁; q₁ -0→ q₂, q₁ -1→ q₀; q₂ -0→ q₁, q₂ -1→ q₂.
'110': q₀ -1→ q₁ -1→ q₀ -0→ q₀. Accept. ✓ (6 mod 3 = 0). '101': q₀ -1→ q₁ -0→ q₂ -1→ q₂. Reject. ✓ (5 mod 3 = 2).

questions.title: NFA to DFA via subset construction
questions.prompt:
Consider the NFA N with states {A, B, C}, alphabet Σ = {0,1}, start state A, accepting states {C}, and transitions:

State01
A{A,B}{A}
B∅{C}
C∅∅

(i) Apply the subset construction to produce an equivalent DFA. Show the construction table systematically, starting from state {A}.
(ii) Identify the accepting states of the resulting DFA and state the language L(N) informally.
questions.hint: Each DFA state is a subset of {A,B,C}. Compute δ({A},0) and δ({A},1) first, then process each newly discovered subset.

markscheme.text: δ({A},0) = {A,B}; δ({A},1) = {A}. δ({A,B},0) = A's 0-transitions ∪ B's 0-transitions = {A,B} ∪ ∅ = {A,B}; δ({A,B},1) = {A} ∪ {C} = {A,C}.
δ({A,C},0) = {A,B} ∪ ∅ = {A,B}; δ({A,C},1) = {A} ∪ ∅ = {A}. No new subsets. DFA states: {A}, {A,B}, {A,C}.
Accepting state: {A,C} (the only DFA state containing C). Language L(N): all strings over {0,1} that end with '01' (a 0 immediately followed by a 1).

questions.title: Regular expressions and closure under complement
questions.prompt: This question is about regular expressions and closure properties.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: Let L be the language over {0,1} of all binary strings containing an even number of 1s (zero counts as even). Write a regular expression for L and briefly explain why it is correct.

markscheme.text: A correct regular expression: 0*(10*10*)* (leading 0s, then zero or more pairs of 1s separated by 0s, with trailing 0s absorbed). Or equivalently: (0|10*1)*.

Explanation: each pair (10*1) contributes exactly two 1s; repeating zero or more times gives an even total; interspersed 0s do not change the count. Any syntactically correct and semantically equivalent regex is accepted.

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Prove that the class of regular languages is closed under complement: if L ⊆ Σ* is regular, then Σ* \ L is also regular.

markscheme.text: Since L is regular, there exists a DFA M = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) with L(M) = L. (A DFA is total: every state has a transition on every symbol.)
Construct M' = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, Q \ F) — identical to M but with accepting and non-accepting states swapped. Then M' accepts w iff M rejects w, so L(M') = Σ* \ L. Since M' is a DFA, Σ* \ L is regular. ✓

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Write a regular expression for the complement of L (i.e. binary strings with an odd number of 1s). Verify it accepts '1' and rejects '11'.

markscheme.text: A correct regular expression: 0*1(0*10*10*)* or equivalently 0*10*(10*10*)*. This matches: any 0s, then exactly one 1, then zero or more pairs of 1s (each separated by 0s), giving an odd total.

Verify '1': 0*(=ε)·1·(0*10*10*)*(=ε). Matches. ✓ (1 one, odd). Verify '11': would need the second '1' to start a pair, giving '11' total = 2 ones (even). The regex requires an even number after the first '1', so '11' has 1 initial + 1 pair-start but no pair-end = fails to match. Rejected. ✓

questions.title: The Pumping Lemma
questions.prompt: This question is about proving languages are not regular.

parts.label: a
parts.prompt: State the Pumping Lemma for regular languages precisely. Include: the pumping length p, the conditions on the decomposition s = xyz, and the conclusion about pumped strings.

markscheme.text: If L is a regular language, then there exists a pumping length p ≥ 1 such that:
for every string s ∈ L with |s| ≥ p, there exists a decomposition s = xyz with (1) |xy| ≤ p, (2) |y| ≥ 1,
(3) for all i ≥ 0, the string xy^i z ∈ L (the string obtained by repeating y i times is still in L).

parts.label: b
parts.prompt: Use the Pumping Lemma to prove that L = {aⁿbⁿ | n ≥ 0} is not regular.

markscheme.text: Assume for contradiction that L is regular with pumping length p. Choose s = aᵖbᵖ ∈ L; |s| = 2p ≥ p.
For any decomposition s = xyz with |xy| ≤ p and |y| ≥ 1: since the first p characters are all a's and |xy| ≤ p, both x and y consist entirely of a's. Write y = aᵏ with 1 ≤ k ≤ p.
Pump with i = 2: xy²z = aᵖ⁺ᵏbᵖ. Since k ≥ 1, there are more a's than b's, so aᵖ⁺ᵏbᵖ ∉ L.
This contradicts condition (3) of the Pumping Lemma. Therefore L is not regular. ✓

parts.label: c
parts.prompt: Use the Pumping Lemma to prove that the language P = {w ∈ {a,b}* | w is a palindrome} is not regular.

markscheme.text: Assume P is regular with pumping length p. Choose s = aᵖbaᵖ ∈ P; |s| = 2p+1 ≥ p. For any decomposition s = xyz with |xy| ≤ p, |y| ≥ 1: the prefix xy lies in the initial run of a's, so y = aᵏ with 1 ≤ k ≤ p.
Pump with i = 2: xy²z = aᵖ⁺ᵏbaᵖ. This has p+k a's before the b and p a's after; since k ≥ 1, it is not a palindrome, so xy²z ∉ P — contradicting the Pumping Lemma. Therefore P is not regular.
